# The Birth of the Stars

Long before time had a name, the Sky was empty and dark, while the Earth below blazed with light. The Sun Goddess, Solara, kept all the light for herself, hoarding it jealously in her golden palace beneath the mountains.

One night, a curious child named Kira climbed the highest peak to see where the light went when darkness fell. She discovered Solara's palace and saw countless glowing gems locked in crystal jars—pieces of light the goddess had stolen from the world.

"Why do you keep all the light trapped?" Kira asked.

"Because light is precious, and I must protect it," Solara replied.

"But light is meant to be shared," said Kira. "What good is treasure that brings joy to no one?"

Her words touched Solara's heart. The goddess realized her greed had created the frightening darkness above. Together, they opened every jar, and Kira threw the glowing gems skyward with all her strength.

Each gem became a star, scattered across the heavens. 

To this day, stars remind us that even the smallest light, when shared, can illuminate the darkest void. And on the clearest nights, you can still see Kira's handprints glowing among the constellations—a child's gift to the world.

Myth: Long after Kira scattered the stars, she grew old and became the Keeper of Dawn. Each morning, she had to decide whether to release the sunrise or keep the world in darkness. Most days, she shared the light freely, painting the sky in gold and crimson.

But one winter, when frost spirits mocked her generosity, Kira grew bitter. "Why should I give freely when others only take?" She held back the dawn for three days, and the world shivered in endless night.

A young fox, brave despite the cold, climbed to her tower. "Keeper, I brought you this," he said, offering a scrap of bread—his only food.

"Why give me your last meal?" Kira asked, ashamed.

"Because sharing keeps us warm when nothing else can," the fox replied.

Kira remembered the lesson she'd once taught Solara. She released the most brilliant sunrise ever seen, and warmth flooded back to the world.

From that day, she never withheld the dawn again. The fox became her companion, and together they ensured each morning arrived faithfully.

The stars still watch over them, glittering witnesses to the eternal truth: generosity returned always shines brighter than generosity given, and the smallest gift given freely can kindle the greatest light.

Myth: Long after Teros fell into his eternal slumber and became the mountains, his sister Mara wandered the earth searching for him. She was the second giant, born from stone and starlight, arriving too late to save her brother from his loneliness.

For decades, Mara traveled, calling his name across valleys and plains. The small creatures watched her pass, but dared not speak of the mountains' secret—that Teros slept beneath their peaks.

One autumn evening, Mara climbed the highest mountain range, exhausted from her search. As she rested against the summit, she felt a familiar heartbeat beneath the stone—slow, steady, ancient. She recognized her brother's presence immediately.

Weeping with joy and sorrow, Mara lay down beside the mountain range, curling her body protectively around the peaks. She whispered, "I found you, brother. You'll never be alone again."

As she closed her eyes, the same transformation claimed her. Her body became hills that embraced the mountains, her tears formed lakes that reflected the sky, her breath became wind that sang through the valleys.

Now the mountains and hills stand together, two giants finally reunited. And when storms rage, some say it's just Mara gently holding her brother close, keeping him warm through the endless years.
